TRANSCRIPT
This is video four in how to manage other people's launches, how to help people
with the launch process. The last one I started off telling you every deal's a deal, which is
some of the best advice you're ever going to get. In other words, everything is negotiable.
Now this one is about how to negotiate and really about what to negotiate.
The number one thing to remember is this is value-based selling. Unless
you're just doing a strictly consulting deal or a coaching deal, and even that, that's value
based. It's like what would a successful launch mean to someone. If it's someone that's
just starting out that maybe wouldn't even be in business, but you can help them do a
$30,000 launch that will set them up with a $100,000 business, then what's it worth to
them, a lot of money; or if someone that has some established business, but doesn't really
know how to do a launch, and maybe they've got the potential to do a $400,000 or
$500,000 or a $1,000,000 launch, what's that worth to them, a lot.
You have to get out of your mind like you don't want to think in terms of dollars per
hour, and boy, I usually got paid $25 an hour, so even quoting $50 an hour is crazy, but if
this deal really comes off, I'll end up getting paid $200 an hour, and that's just insane. I
could never consider that, or it'd end up being $1,000 an hour. You have to look at what's
the value going to be to them. You've left behind the civilian world of regular normal
dollars per hours when you do this, because this launch mile is so leveraged and you're
bringing huge leverage to them, so remember that. No one says like when you show up to
put your money, deposit a check in the bank, they're not going to say did you work really
hard for that, how many hours did you actually put in for that $12,000 check. That's just
ridiculous. You did not put in enough hours so we're not going to cash the check. They're
not going to do that. You need to get paid on the value.
One other thing, I'm going to get real high tech and draw out a little diagram for
you. This is the diagram of how much they love you. We're going to have this axis here as
this is how much they love you. As you move up the axis here, they love you more. When
you start out and you sign this deal, maybe they love you this amount. That's how much
they love you. Then after that, you're immediately going to start making them work
because that's part of your job is getting them to do work, and probably work harder than
they expected. Your job is going to be able to push them, push them to create a great offer
to start with, then to create a great Prelaunch. They're probably going to start loving you

less. As you ask them to work more and more, they're going to love you less and less and

Now you're going to be going along and then you're going to get into the start of

Then they start to see it come all together and they're going to start to love you more
because it's like they can feel this is actually happening, it's real. We're getting opt ins.
We're getting comments. People are excited about these videos. Then your Prelaunch goes
along and it still seems like it's going and they're loving you more and more. Then you
open cart right here. The sales start coming in. You open cart. What happens there, you
guessed it. They love you a lot more. All of a sudden they love you a ton. They love you a
ton because these sales are coming in. Then you go through your open cart and then you
get into at the end of the open cart, and this is the final rush, and it gets positively
euphoric. This is close cart.
I don't know if you're going to be able to read these but it's for my edification here.
They love you. I mean the sales are pouring in. You're amazing. You're amazing, and then
they have the day after cart and you got a little bit left over euphoria. You know what
happens after that? They start to love you less and it goes like this. It starts to fall off. I'm
actually out of room so it's going to be compressed. Let's just pretend these last little bit is
the final 30 days and it starts to come down here. Then it's time to pay you, and it goes all
the way through here because they even, no matter how honorable and awesome they are,
it becomes a question of what have you done for me lately, what have you done for me
lately.
Let's just talk about say like a $1,000,000 launch. Right here they just closed and
it's $1,000,000, like unbelievable. They're just so stoked. They never dreamed of a
$1,000,000 launch.
Then what happens is they have a bunch of refunds come in. All of a sudden
$100,000 launch becomes a $900,000 launch. Let's just say they've agreed to pay you
15% so they're going to pay you $150,000. All of a sudden they're $1,000,000 launch
becomes a $900,000 launch because of refunds. Then they have some payments that
don't go through, so all of a sudden it becomes an $850,000 launch.
Then they have some partners to pay, and so their $850,000 launch all of a sudden
becomes a $450,000 launch. Then they have some costs in their business. They got some
hosting costs and some team costs and some whatever. You do a $1,000,000 project

there's going to be costs. All of a sudden their $450,000 becomes $350,000.
By now they're neck deep in actually all the fulfillment, and all the customer
service, and all the complications that comes from servicing the people who bought a
$1,000,000 launch. They have their $350,000 but they owe you $150,000. They think
wow, I going to pay them $150,000 and that leaves me with $200,000, and I'm doing all
this work and I'm dealing with all these headaches that come from fulfilling on this. They
made $150,000 and I made only $200,000. That doesn't seem fair. Not every client is
going to go through this but a lot of them are. Trust me. That's where they love you a lot
less. I guess it's just human nature but this is what tends to happen.
What's really important is that you negotiate stuff when they're a little bit more in
love with you here. I mean right here. It'd be great if you could negotiate when they're
completely off the charts in love with you, but at least you want to get when they're
moderately in love with you is when you want to negotiate something. First of all, you
want to think something that's easy to negotiate ahead of time but hard after. If you want
to get access to the shopping cart, you want to have the log in details so you can actually
watch the orders coming in, so you can verify what the numbers are for the launch. You
don't want to just depend on them telling you what the numbers are because most people
are honest, but not everyone's honest. When you can actually look in the cart and say this
is exact number, then it's clear for everyone exactly what the sales were and exactly what
you should get paid.
Another way you could do this, this is a little harder but I've seen this done, the
money actually flows through you. If you've got a merchant account, the orders come into
your account. Then you take your percentage and then you pay them. Now most deals are
not done this way, but I have seen it done. If you look at the book world does it this way.
In the book world, you took real physical books, real publishers that have been doing this
for centuries, the money goes through them. The royalties go through them. They take out
their piece and then they send you the check, so that's something to consider. One thing is
I have seen $1,000,000 launches where they do the $1,000,000 launch, all of a sudden
there's no money left over. Somehow all the money disappears.
You want to negotiate when you get paid. Hopefully it's X percent of it within a
week or two, and the balance within 30 days, or something along that lines. Be very clear
on when the money gets paid. What I talk about this I mean all the money, the upfront
money, the retainer money, but especially the percentage of the launch money. One thing
you want to negotiate is relaunches. If you go ahead and put together this launch, the

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
beautiful thing about it is most of the hard work is done. Generally, you can do a relaunch
six months, or nine months or a year later, and there's almost no extra work for either you
or your partner. One thing is if you're going to just do all that work upfront, and then
they're able to do the relaunch without compensating on that, that's fine. We said, "Every
deal's a deal," but then you should probably get a bigger chunk upfront.
I think a better way to do it is you get paid on the initial launch and you also get
paid on the relaunch. Maybe on the relaunch you get a lower percentage. I don't know.
Every deal's a deal. You can negotiate anything you want, but do not do the deal without
at least thinking about the relaunch and how you get paid on the relaunch, or whether you
get paid on the relaunch. It's a huge item to negotiate, and a lot of people never even think
about the relaunch. The reality is you're putting up so much work upfront, and that work
is going to be reusable. Think about the relaunch.
I've already talked about the different types of fees, the retainer, the upfront, the
percentage of the launch. Another thing that could be on the table is equity,
actually having an ownership piece of the business. Having an ownership piece
of the list that is generated is another option. Getting a mailing, like maybe you say you're
helping someone. It's your neighbor and your neighbor's going to do a product about
training horses. Say sometimes maybe you have a business about training horses as well.
I've seen this type of thing happen a lot where someone who's already in the market helps
someone else. Maybe you can negotiate that you'll have a launch list when you come out
of that launch, and that launch list is going to be red hot super warm list. Maybe you
negotiate a mailing, or two mailings, or three mailings into your business, into your
product, into your next launch, something to put on the table and to think about.
Finally, every deal's a deal and it's much, much easier to negotiate upfront. You
want to get all these things as crystal clear as possible. I'm a fan of regular English
language instead of legalese, that you actually negotiate something that's readable by
regular humans, not by lawyers. Also, another great thing to put in there is how is your
relationship going to end. What does the end of your relationship look like? What's the
out clause for each of you? This is something that can probably protect both of you. Then
you have a lot of other legal stuff like what courts it's going to be settled in and all that
stuff. I'm not a lawyer. I'm not going to tell you about that. I'm just giving you the big
picture of the things you have to talk about - things like the shopping cart access, and
when you get paid, and how the money flows, and how do the relaunches work.
One more big thing that goes along with that relaunch is the back end because a lot

of times the launch sets up another sale for a higher end product down the road, or maybe
